guide_html:
HƯỚNG DẪN VIẾT BÀI

Pie chart \+ Table: Anthropology graduates

1. Giải thích đề

Biểu đồ tròn: tình trạng việc làm của sinh viên ngành Nhân học sau khi tốt nghiệp.

Bảng: mức lương sau 5 năm theo loại công việc.

→ Dạng: mixed (outcomes \+ comparison)

2. Introduction (paraphrase)

Giữ:

destination of graduates

salaries after five years

Mẫu:

The pie chart illustrates the destinations of anthropology graduates from one university, while the table shows their salary distribution after five years of work across different employment sectors.

3. Overview (ý chính)

Phần lớn sinh viên có việc làm full-time

Unemployment và postgraduate chiếm tỷ lệ nhỏ

Sau 5 năm, mức lương cao ($75k+) chiếm phần lớn

Government sector có tỷ lệ lương cao nhất

4. BODY 1 – Pie chart (destinations)

Full-time work: 52% (lớn nhất)

Part-time work: 15%

Unemployed: 12%

Full-time postgraduate study: 8%

Not known: 8%

Part-time work \+ study: 5%

→ Ý chính:

  • Hơn một nửa có việc full-time
  • Các lựa chọn khác chiếm tỷ lệ nhỏ hơn nhiều

5. BODY 2 – Table (salaries after 5 years)

Freelance consultants:

$75–99k: 40%

$100k+: 40% (cao)

Government sector:

$100k+: 50% (cao nhất overall)

$75–99k: 30%

Private companies:

$50–74k: 35% (cao nhất nhóm này)

$100k+: 30%

→ Ý chính:

  • Freelance & government có nhiều người lương cao
  • Private tập trung ở mức trung bình

6. Gợi ý từ vựng

enter full-time employment

account for the largest proportion

a relatively small percentage

earn a higher salary

be concentrated in

be more likely to

The charts show the destinations of anthropology graduates from one university and their salaries after five years of work.

Just over half of graduates (52%) entered full-time employment, making it the most common outcome. Part-time work accounted for 15%, while 5% combined part-time work with postgraduate study. A further 8% pursued full-time postgraduate study. Unemployment stood at 12%, and outcomes for 8% of graduates were not known.

Regarding salaries after five years, earnings varied by employment type. In the government sector, the largest proportion of graduates (50%) earned over $100,000, followed by 30% in the $75,000–99,999 range. Freelance consultants also performed strongly, with 80% earning at least $75,000, evenly split between the top two bands (40% each). In contrast, those working in private companies were more concentrated in the mid-range, with 35% earning $50,000–74,999 and 30% exceeding $100,000. Lower salary bands ($25,000–49,999) were relatively uncommon across all sectors, accounting for only 5–10% of graduates.
